Regular Article

Advancing an inclusive research approach in humanities and social sciences: The crucial role of self-study and narrative enquiry in doctoral research experiences
==================================================================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Magdalena Kohout-Diaz 1

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssaho.2024.101256](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ssaho.2024.101256 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2590291124004534&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

This article examines the potential of self-study and narrative enquiry as inclusive research methodologies within the realm of higher education, with a particular emphasis on the doctoral experience. The lived experiences of individuals who have faced discrimination during their academic pursuits are illuminated through the analysis of two distinct case studies. The intersectionality of discrimination is emphasised, underscoring the necessity for higher education institutions to ensure equitable, and inclusive practices. A sustained effort to challenge and transform discriminatory practices and power dynamics is advocated, not only within the research process but also in the wider contexts where research is undertaken. This endeavour necessitates a profound comprehension of the intricate nuances inherent in the concept of inclusion, accompanied by a resolute dedication to acknowledging the distinctive requirements of each individual. The objective is to contribute to the ongoing discourse on inclusive research methodologies and provide a foundation for future research and initiatives aimed at fostering justice and ethical practices within higher educational institutions.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004625)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004583)

Keywords
--------

Inclusive research

Doctoral supervision

Self-study

Narrative inquiry

Research ethics

1. Introduction
---------------

Many research methodologies in the field of education, which espouse the principles of objectivity and universal applicability, adopt an exclusively top-down approach. These approaches fail to take account of the researcher's own experience, which is considered to be the subject under investigation, in the context of the specific contexts in which the investigation takes place. Building on the insights gained from [Berger (2015)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib4) on the researcher's position and reflexivity in qualitative research, this article aims to reflect on the challenges posed by the human element in ethical inclusive considerations in scientific research in the humanities and social sciences ([Kohout-Diaz & Deyrich, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib27)).

The researcher's position and involvement in the investigation are of decisive importance, especially when the subject is of personal importance to the researcher ([Morin, 1992](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib32)). An inclusive research methodology enables the involvement of individuals at the centre of the research, in conjunction with the researcher's specific involvement. This approach is founded upon an ethical scientific commitment ([Guðjónsdóttir et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib18)) to social justice and the capacity to combat any form of segregation ([Sensoy & DiAngelo, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib44)), including intersectional discrimination.

The epistemological framework of the article is based on self-learning and narrative enquiry methodologies, which have been demonstrated to be valid in the field of education ([Loughran et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib30); Clandinin, 2006; [Craig & Curtis, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib10)). Adaptations have been made to accommodate the doctoral context and the progressive objective of self-learning. This involves identifying problems through reflection on one's own experience, with the aim of developing improvement objectives based on this work ([La Boskey, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib29)). The design of the research activity is therefore considered both for its intrinsic value as a means of producing data and as an ongoing process of reflective self-training for the researcher, in accordance with socio constructivist principles ([Vygotsky, 1978](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib46)).

This innovative perspective aims to contribute to the existing debates on educational research methodologies driven by an inclusive ethic by addressing several key questions: (1) What are the prerequisites or necessary conditions for developing and implementing an inclusive research methodology that is both empirically based and committed to social justice and the fight against all forms of segregation? (2) How is it linked to self-training? (3) What contributions and limitations does it present in relation to the theme of discriminatory practices in the doctoral supervision of students, particularly foreign students and women? By answering these questions, this article seeks to contribute to redefine scientific excellence. Rather than valorising strategies of social domination, scientific excellence should be defined as the valorisation of approaches that are inclusive, ethical and open to diversity ([Kohout-Diaz & Deyrich, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib27)).

Three hypotheses will help to understand the multiple facets of discrimination, the prevalence of excessive entitlement among faculty members, and the desire for more inclusive practices mentioned above. The first hypothesis concerns the presence of discriminatory biases in recruitment and evaluation processes. These biases can mask the qualifications and academic achievements of candidates, particularly African women (intersectionality). Often institutionalised, they have a profound impact on the careers of academics. Influenced by discriminatory prejudices based on applicants' foreign surnames or ethnic origin, as well as their gender, these biases lead to unfair treatment and prevent foreign female doctoral candidates from obtaining academic positions despite their high qualifications. The second hypothesis concerns the impact of discrimination on the university experience. Foreign doctoral students' experiences of discrimination and prejudice have a negative impact on their careers, leading to feelings of exclusion, self-censorship, lower self-esteem and academic self-efficacy. They contribute to the creation of a hostile and non-inclusive university environment.

Finally, the third hypothesis concerns resilience (particularly through reflective self-study). Despite facing significant discrimination and prejudice, discriminated doctoral students can demonstrate resilience, creativity and resourcefulness and remain committed to their studies.

**Part 1: Define inclusive approaches in the context of humanities and social sciences research.**

The objective of this section is to examine the characteristics of socially just and non-segregative research. To achieve this, the concept of inclusive research will be analysed, with particular attention paid to the challenges associated with the concept of inclusion and the importance of considering each individual case in the pursuit of the inclusion of all and of everyone at the same time. Supporting an inclusive conviction means advocating for a scientific ethic and avoiding the excesses of positive discrimination, which, by defining itself as a compensatory struggle, risks creating new forms of discrimination. In contrast, the conceptualisation of some research as inclusive is part of a concern to democratise and open up the research process to all ([Appadurai, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib2)). This is a fight for social justice, both in and through research ([Seale et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib43), p. 347). The aforementioned reflections will be deployed in the specific context of work on excessive faculty teacher entitlement ([Craig & Ratnam, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib11), pp. 273–283) which will also be described.

### 1.1. The characteristics of inclusive research as either a fight against discrimination or as a fight for the ethical quality of scientific practices

A number of studies have already examined the necessity and the transformative potential of inclusive research methodologies. For example, [Seale et al. (2014)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib43) and [Nind (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib33) emphasise, in line with [Appadurai (2013)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib2), the significance of advancing social justice in the scientific practices and democratising the research process. In particular, [Nind (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib33) emphasises the practical wisdom and collective transformation involved in inclusive research. [Nind & Lewthwaite (2018)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib34) apply the concept of inclusive pedagogy to the teaching of research methods, emphasising the necessity for pragmatic and comprehensive pedagogical approaches. For their part, [Strnadová et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib45) examine a range of inclusive research methodologies, with a particular focus on the experiences of individuals with disabilities.

The aforementioned authors also demonstrate that research of the so-called inclusive quality (or at least that which aspires to this quality) is subject to numerous challenges ([Katzenbach, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib23); [Pawlak et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib36)). Does this type of research aim to guarantee that all individuals, irrespective of background, race, gender, sexual orientation, religion, socioeconomic status or disability, are afforded an equal opportunity to engage in and derive benefit from the research process? Alternatively, should particular attention be paid to groups that have been subjected to discrimination? This raises the question of whether it would be preferable to adopt an inclusive approach that encompasses all groups, or whether it would be more beneficial to focus on a smaller, marginalised group. What is the optimal solution to this ethical dilemma?

The aforementioned papers have situated these issues within the field of higher education, with a particular focus on the context of research. The collective findings of these studies underscore the imperative for the implementation of research methodologies that facilitate the advancement of social justice in the field. This is a current scientific concern that aims to redefine what is typically considered scientific excellence, as well as the variation in the definition of excellence and in the evaluative practices in decision-making fora ([O'Connor & O'Hagan, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib35)).

### 1.2. The concept of inclusion: specific problems and dilemmas

As previously stated, the concept of inclusion is not without its inherent challenges. One of the primary challenges associated with this concept is its polysemy, or the existence of multiple meanings or interpretations. To illustrate, the concept of inclusion is not merely a matter of combating discrimination; it implies a more global and positive approach. The objective is to guarantee accessibility for all, while considering the distinctive attributes of each individual. In some contexts, the concept of inclusion is understood as the mere co-presence or representation of diverse groups in research, without any further consideration of their specific needs. In other contexts, the concept of inclusion can be interpreted as the active involvement and meaningful participation of these groups in the research process. In some contexts, the term ‘inclusion’ may be used to describe a state, whereas in others it may be employed to denote an ongoing process ([Kohout-Diaz, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib24)). The multifaceted nature of the concept of inclusion may give rise to confusion and misinterpretation, which could ultimately result in the failure to achieve the goal of a truly inclusive quality of research, education or training. This is due to the fact that the aforementioned meanings are related to worldviews and epistemologies that are not always aligned and may even be in opposition or conflict with one another.

This is particularly true in cases where the question is whether or not the concept of singularity is inseparable from that of the universality of inclusive processes. A scientific practice that is accessible to all is a perspective of autonomous appropriation that is open to an infinite variety of viewpoints and ways of doing things. As [Feyerabend (2010)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib14) demonstrates, the humanities and social sciences must adopt an opportunistic approach to methodology. This entails acknowledging that no research methodology can be dismissed outright ([Horsthemke, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib21)), while refraining from reducing individuals to mere objects of study and thought or action in their stead, even when such actions are driven by benevolent and inclusive intentions.

Moreover, the universalist nature of the concept of inclusion can, on occasion, result in the overlooking of the distinctive characteristics of each particular situation. A “for all” approach carries the risk of failing to consider the specific needs and experiences of “everyone”. The fundamental premise is that the universal can be attained through a case-by-case analysis, with due consideration given to the singular ([Kohout-Diaz, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib24)). It is only through an understanding of and consideration for the unique experiences and needs of each individual or group that true inclusion can be achieved ([Pawlak et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib36)). The subject is best positioned to evaluate and categorise their particular circumstances and requirements through the process of self-reflection.

### 1.3. The context of the excessive faculty entitlement phenomenon in doctoral studies

In the context of doctoral supervision, the pursuit of social justice, inclusive approaches and the fight against all forms of discrimination assume particular importance. Research has demonstrated that doctoral students, particularly those from marginalised or under-represented groups, can encounter specific challenges and barriers throughout their academic careers.

One factor that can intensify these challenges and contribute to inequalities in the PhD experience is the tendency of some PhD supervisors to display excessive entitlement.

In the demanding context of doctoral support and supervision, invoking the concept of excessive faculty entitlement is intended to “identify and better describe situations that are obstacles to the development of inclusive, good quality education for all and a fair, non-discriminatory education concerned with respect for diversity” ([Kohout-Diaz & Deyrich, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib26), p. 54). It is imperative to develop “supervision practices and development that recognizes and responds to diversity (e.g., developmental variations, neurodiversities, race/ethnicity, language, gender, sexual orientation, economic class, country of origin, culture, community, and certain interests) in order to support others in seeing the brilliance of all students.” ([Baker et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib3), p. 2). The implementation of innovative, equitable and empathetic approaches to supervision has the potential to facilitate the creation of a more inclusive and effective educational environment. These approaches serve to elucidate pathways towards a supervisory model that is not a means of control, but rather a tool for empowerment, growth and collective success.

This raises the question of the optimal methodology for collecting and processing this sensitive data. From an epistemological point of view, doctoral students must be given the opportunity to describe their experience themselves. From an ethical point of view, young researchers, who are often in a difficult position vis-à-vis their thesis supervisor, must be respected and care must be taken not to aggravate their situation, even if these practices must be denounced.

This chapter has explored the notion of socially just and inclusive research. However, the concept of inclusion is complex and open to multiple interpretations, which can lead to complex challenges and misunderstandings. For example, the universalist nature of the concept of inclusion can sometimes lead to neglecting the uniqueness of each situation or focusing solely on combating stigmatisation. A superficial, rhetorical or partial “for all” strategy sometimes risks neglecting the specific needs and experiences of the individual “for each”. The way out of these dilemmas is to consider that universal law is actualised through an equitable case-by-case analysis, through the singular. In the context of doctoral supervision, which is particularly exposed to the risk of discrimination, and where excessive expectations on the part of supervisors can exacerbate these challenges and contribute to inequalities, this is precisely what justifies the use of a narrative and self-reflexive approach, in which each young researcher is invited to bear witness to their own experience, in their own words.

In order to better understand this coherence between the research objectives and the methodology used, it is now appropriate to examine the concept of reflective self-learning on a case-by-case basis in inclusive educational research. The aim is to explore self-learning both as a tool for personal and professional development and as an inclusive research strategy for promoting social justice and combating discrimination in the scientific and academic environment.

**Part 2: Epistemological foundations and methods**.

This section explores the relevance of the narrative self-study in education research and its inclusivity and its application in understanding and addressing issues of discrimination in doctoral supervision. It also presents the design of the survey, a corpus of testimonies from doctoral students who have experienced discrimination (including information on the participants' backgrounds), the data collection process and the data analysis, with the aim of providing a detailed illustration of the aforementioned concepts..[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#fn2)

### 1.4. What do we mean by narrative self-studies in education?

Self-study methodology is becoming increasingly common in educational research. This approach assumes that a comprehensive understanding of educational practices and contexts can only be achieved by examining an individual's personal experiences within these contexts ([La Boskey, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib29); [Loughran et al., 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib30)). It is a “study of the self and others in practice” ([Pinnegar & Hamilton, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib38), p. 12) which involves a critical examination of one's own practices and those of others in the same educational context.

The objective of self-study research is not merely the generation of knowledge. This entails a “moral commitment to improving practice” ([Hauge, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib19)), signifying that self-study researchers are not mere passive observers, but active participants in the educational processes they study. They are dedicated to utilising the insights derived from their research to refine their own practices and contribute to the enhancement of the educational contexts in which they operate. It is therefore relevant to use these methods to improve the inclusive quality of doctoral supervision practice, both for supervisors and for academic institutions.

One approach that has been demonstrated to be particularly efficacious in facilitating self-study research in the context of doctoral supervision is individual narrative inquiry. As outlined by [Clandinin (2022)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib8), this approach sheds light on the phenomenon of excessive faculty entitlement in supervision through an examination of personal experiences. The foundation of narrative inquiry is the acknowledgement that humans employ narratives to derive meaning from their experiences ([Craig, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib9)). The process entails the collection and analysis of narratives or accounts of individuals' experiences, with the objective of attaining a more profound comprehension of these experiences and the meanings ascribed to them by the individuals in question.

As [Clandinin (2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib8), p. 7) asserts, narrative enquiry is an approach to the study of human lives that is designed to facilitate the recognition of lived experience as a source of meaningful knowledge and understanding. In the context of doctoral supervision, this entails acknowledging and respecting the lived experiences of doctoral students and supervisors and utilising these experiences as a source of insight into the dynamics of doctoral supervision.

### 1.5. The method of narrative case study and subsequent analysis is a particularly inclusive approach to research in education. Why is this?

This study employs a case-by-case methodology, drawing upon critical and self-reflexive ethnographic techniques as practised in the field of education and training ([Kohout-Diaz, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib25), pp. 105–106). The approach is centred on the act of self-narration by each respondent within the context of their own subjective, cultural, historical, linguistic and other experiences. The benefit of this approach is that it places greater emphasis on the individual's own practice of enhancing their knowledge than on affirming knowledge that aligns with established, external standards within the field ([Craig & Curtis, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib10), p. 27). The methodology employed in this study combines self-directed reflexive learning and narrative inquiry, with an emphasis on the experiential dimension. This concept is firmly established in the work of Dewey ([Dewey, 1938](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib12)): it is the individual who determines the terminology that most accurately represents their experience. This approach may be regarded as the epitome of ethical and inclusive practice. A tension between epistemology (the examination of one's knowledge and the ways of naming things) and ontology (the examination of being) can be observed in this process of reflective narrative enquiry.

The aim of self-directed study and learning in the field of educational research is to achieve a more profound comprehension of phenomena, while simultaneously facilitating personal growth and development. As [Flyvbjerg (2001](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib15), p. 167) and [Craig & Curtis (2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib10), p. 30) observe, research in teacher education is conducted with the objective of facilitating a collective understanding of the present circumstances, the desired future state, and the values and interests to be pursued in order to achieve that future state. The objective is to furnish the means to attain these goals by cultivating personal and collective professionalism, as well as enhancing practice, to a degree commensurate with the accumulation of scientific data ([Hauge, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib19)).

The process of exploring ethical tensions requires participants to adopt an attitude of honesty and a willingness to confront their own strengths and weaknesses ([Guðjónsdóttir et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib18), p. 59). It is incumbent upon researchers to critically examine their professional values, to strive for a more nuanced understanding of themselves, and to pursue a moral purpose ([Hauge, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590291124004534#bib19)). Our research is firmly rooted in the methodological framework described above. This method, which incorporates dimensions of reflexivity, personal engagement, analysis and interpretation of socio-historical contexts, and sensitive writing toward

enriched_points.expanded_content: Qualitative research involves subjective interpretations and dynamic contexts, making it challenging to ensure validity, reliability, and generalizability of findings (Saraswati, 2025).

Researchers face challenges in protecting their intellectual contributions and ensuring proper attribution in collaborative and open-access environments, raising ethical dilemmas in knowledge sharing (Holloway, 2007).
Maintaining participant anonymity and confidentiality is crucial, yet difficult, especially in qualitative studies involving sensitive topics or marginalized groups (Sanjari, 2014).

Scholarly communities often resist adopting open science practices due to entrenched traditions, fear of losing control over research outputs, and skepticism regarding new methodologies (Reuber, 2022).
Limited funding for qualitative research restricts access to resources necessary for conducting comprehensive studies, impacting the scope and quality of research outputs (Kohout-Diaz, 2024).
